name: CommandCC — Military C2-Inspired AI Agent Orchestration for Software Development
origin_domain: Software engineering (AI agent orchestration, military-inspired C2 doctrine)
year_developed: 2026 (v0.1.0 Genesis release April 3, 2026; active development through 2026)
driving_insight: A single human operator commanding a single AI agent is throughput-limited: one agent can only do one thing at a time, and the human becomes the bottleneck for task decomposition and quality review. Military organizations solved a similar problem centuries ago with the Command and Control (C2) doctrine: a commander delegates to a hierarchical staff organization where each echelon has defined responsibilities, communication protocols, and decision authority. CommandCC directly transplants this doctrine to AI agent orchestration: 4-128 AI agents organized in a 3-tier hierarchy (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku mirroring Command/Operations/Recon echelons) execute a 9-phase software development pipeline (RECON → STRATEGY → DECOMPOSE → ARCH → BUILD → WIRE → TEST → REVIEW → INTEGRATE), with each phase fanning out into parallel agents and collapsing back into a shared artifact before the next phase. The Decomposer (a single Opus agent between Strategy and Architecture) is the 'force multiplier': it takes one objective and breaks it into independent sub-objectives with zero file/component overlap, enabling fully parallel downstream execution. A human operator types 4 sentences of intent and 16 agents deploy 4 features with 131 passing tests in 13 minutes — a 200x cognitive load compression. The driving insight is that software development, like warfare, is a coordination problem: the bottleneck is not code generation speed but task decomposition quality, dependency management, and quality assurance — and military C2 doctrine already solved these coordination problems.
decision_style: Hierarchical with role-based specialization. The 3-tier hierarchy mirrors military echelons: COMMAND tier (Opus) makes strategic decisions — what to build, how to decompose, architecture design, quality review. OPERATIONS tier (Sonnet) makes tactical decisions — how to implement, wire, and test specific components. RECON tier (Haiku) makes reconnaissance decisions — what to scan, what patterns to detect, what to report. Decisions flow downward (COMMAND → OPERATIONS) as orders and upward (OPERATIONS → COMMAND, RECON → COMMAND) as reports. This is fundamentally hierarchical-military decision-making: the commander sets intent and decomposes, subordinates execute within their authority. The Decomposer (Opus) is the key decision-maker: it determines sub-objective boundaries to ensure zero overlap, enabling parallel execution. This is a DELIBERATIVE decision (Opus with extended thinking analyzes the codebase and objective), not algorithmic (there is no automatic decomposition algorithm). Verification decisions (REVIEW phase) are also deliberative: Opus reviewers judge PASS/NEEDS CHANGES for each component. The human operator makes the top-level strategic decision (what to build, expressed as 4 sentences of intent) and retains authority over money, accounts, DNS, and external services (Fleet Doctrine Rule 8: halt on gaps, never assume autonomy).
problem_class_targeted: Multi-file, multi-feature software development tasks that can be decomposed into independent sub-objectives. The problem class is: (1) large — requiring changes across multiple files, components, or services; (2) decomposable — the objective can be split into sub-objectives with zero file/component overlap (the Decomposer's guarantee); (3) parallelizable — sub-objectives can be built, tested, and reviewed independently; (4) quality-sensitive — all changes must pass automated tests and human-quality review; (5) coordination-intensive — sub-objectives must integrate coherently at the end. CommandCC targets the gap between 'single-agent coding assistant' (one agent, one task at a time) and 'full autonomous development' (no human in the loop). It is designed for tasks where a human can describe the objective in natural language but the implementation requires coordinated multi-agent effort. The problem class specifically requires decomposability — tasks that cannot be decomposed into zero-overlap sub-objectives are not suitable for CommandCC's parallel wave model.
ordered_phases: Nine sequential phases, each fanning out into parallel agents, collapsing into a shared artifact before the next phase begins. This is the 'Octopus architecture' — a central body (sequential phase pipeline) with tentacles (parallel agents within each phase): Phase 0 — RECON: Haiku scouts perform read-only codebase scanning (~30 seconds each), building a shared understanding of the codebase structure, existing patterns, and potential impact areas; Phase 1 — STRATEGY: a single Opus strategist receives the RECON reports and human intent, formulates the overall approach, identifies key architectural decisions, and produces a strategy document; Phase 2 — DECOMPOSE: a single Opus decomposer (the 'Force Multiplier') takes the strategy and breaks the objective into independent sub-objectives, guaranteeing zero file/component/dependency overlap between any two sub-objectives — this is the critical phase that enables all downstream parallelism; Phase 3 — ARCH: multiple Opus architects work in parallel, each designing the architecture for one or more sub-objectives — data models, interfaces, component boundaries; Phase 4 — BUILD: multiple Sonnet builders implement the architected sub-objectives in parallel, generating code, tests, and documentation; Phase 5 — WIRE: multiple Sonnet wirers connect the built components — integration code, API wiring, dependency injection, configuration; Phase 6 — TEST: multiple Sonnet testers validate the wired components in parallel — unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests; Phase 7 — REVIEW: multiple Opus reviewers perform quality gates on the tested components — each component is judged PASS or NEEDS CHANGES; Phase 8 — INTEGRATE: a single Opus integrator consolidates all reviewed components into a final BATTLE-MAP.md — a single document showing what was built, how components relate, test results, and any open issues. The forward flow is strictly sequential: each phase must complete before the next begins. Within each phase, agents operate in parallel. This is the 'wave' model — each phase is a wave of parallel agents, and waves proceed sequentially.
feedback_loops: The REVIEW phase (Phase 7) provides a quality feedback loop: components judged NEEDS CHANGES are sent back for revision (back to BUILD for the specific sub-objective). This is an explicit, designed feedback loop — the Opus reviewer's judgment triggers rework of individual components. The INTEGRATE phase (Phase 8) provides an integration feedback loop: if components do not integrate cleanly, the Opus integrator identifies the conflicts and can trigger targeted rework. The wave-based architecture supports partial replanning: if a component fails REVIEW, only that component's sub-objective is reworked, not the entire plan. The fleet doctrine provides a knowledge-sharing feedback loop: 'Grep a fleet peer before improvising on uncovered scope' (Rule 7) — agents share information through the shared codebase and BATTLE-MAP. There is no global replanning loop — the strategy and decomposition are set at the start and not revisited. If the Decomposer produces a flawed decomposition, the downstream phases will discover the flaw but cannot restructure the decomposition.
gates: Two primary gates: Phase 7 REVIEW — Opus reviewers apply a PASS/NEEDS CHANGES judgment to each component; this is a quality gate with specific criteria (code correctness, test coverage, adherence to architecture, documentation completeness). Components that fail must be reworked before integration. This is the primary quality assurance mechanism. Phase 8 INTEGRATE — the Opus integrator performs a final integration gate: do all components work together? Are there conflicts, missing interfaces, or inconsistent assumptions? This is a correctness gate. Additional implicit gates: the Decomposer's zero-overlap guarantee acts as a dependency gate (no two sub-objectives conflict); the TEST phase (Phase 6) acts as a validation gate (all tests must pass). There are no human approval gates within the pipeline — the human provides intent at the start and receives the BATTLE-MAP at the end, but does not approve intermediate phases. Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 overrides: anything that costs money, touches an external account, or changes DNS requires human approval — this is an explicit financial/security gate enforced by doctrine, not architecture.
exit_criteria: The pipeline completes when: (1) all phases 0-8 have completed; (2) all components have passed REVIEW (PASS judgment); (3) all tests pass (TEST phase); (4) the INTEGRATE phase produces a clean BATTLE-MAP.md with no unresolved conflicts. Partial completion: if some components fail REVIEW after multiple rework attempts, they are flagged in the BATTLE-MAP as incomplete with status and reason. The BATTLE-MAP serves as the exit artifact — it documents what was done, what was not done, and why. The human operator reviews the BATTLE-MAP to determine if the overall objective was achieved. Failure exits: (1) RECON fails to understand the codebase (insufficient context, unfamiliar patterns); (2) DECOMPOSE produces overlapping sub-objectives (defeats parallelism); (3) BUILD produces code that cannot pass TEST after multiple iterations; (4) INTEGRATE discovers irreconcilable component conflicts.
decomposition_method: Hierarchical, Opus-driven decomposition with a hard zero-overlap constraint. The Decomposer (Phase 2) is a single Opus agent (with extended thinking) that sits between Strategy and Architecture. Its method: (1) receives the Strategy document (overall approach, architectural decisions); (2) analyzes the codebase structure (files, components, dependencies) identified during RECON; (3) decomposes the objective into N sub-objectives, each mapped to specific files/components; (4) guarantees zero overlap — no file, component, or dependency appears in more than one sub-objective; (5) outputs a decomposition document listing sub-objectives with their file assignments. This is dependency-driven decomposition with a hard boundary constraint: the primary goal is clean separation (zero overlap), not optimal granularity. The Decomposer's decisions are irrevocable — downstream phases execute the decomposition as-is. The zero-overlap guarantee is the key innovation: it enables fully parallel downstream execution because no two agents will ever touch the same file. This is more restrictive than DAG-based decomposition (RDD, JORDAN parallel executor) which allows dependencies — CommandCC eliminates dependencies entirely at the decomposition level, trading expressiveness for parallelism. The Decomposer is called the 'Force Multiplier' because a 4-sentence human objective can explode into 12 sub-objectives → 60+ downstream agents.
granularity_guidance: Granularity is determined by the Decomposer's zero-overlap constraint. A sub-objective's granularity is defined by its file assignment — a sub-objective that touches 1-3 files is a leaf node; a sub-objective that touches 10+ files may need further decomposition. The Decomposer balances: (1) fine-grained — more sub-objectives → more parallelism → faster execution; (2) coarse-grained — fewer sub-objectives → less coordination overhead → lower integration risk. The optimal granularity is influenced by agent tier assignment: Opus agents handle complex architectural sub-objectives; Sonnet agents handle implementation sub-objectives; Haiku agents handle bulk/pattern sub-objectives. The Decomposer's output implicitly defines granularity thresholds: a sub-objective assigned to a Haiku agent is simpler (granular enough for pattern-matching); a sub-objective assigned to an Opus architect is more complex (requires deeper reasoning). The zero-overlap constraint prevents infinite decomposition — you cannot split a sub-objective further without creating overlap or destroying coherence.
boundary_rules: The Decomposer's zero-overlap constraint is the primary boundary rule: no file, component, or dependency overlaps between any two sub-objectives. This is a hard boundary enforced at the decomposition stage, not at execution. The boundary rule guarantees: (1) no merge conflicts — two agents never edit the same file; (2) no dependency deadlocks — sub-objectives are independent; (3) clean handoffs — each agent owns its file set exclusively. The boundary rule is stronger than MECE (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) — it is MECE with the additional constraint that exhaustiveness (all required files are assigned) is the Decomposer's responsibility. Boundary violations (overlapping sub-objectives) are not automatically detected — the Decomposer's guarantee is based on Opus reasoning quality, not algorithmic verification. If the Decomposer makes an error, downstream phases may discover it during INTEGRATE (component conflicts) or during TEST (unexpected interactions). The Fleet Doctrine Rule 7 ('Grep a fleet peer before improvising on uncovered scope') provides a runtime boundary check — agents consult each other's work before touching uncovered files.
risk_assessment_mechanism: No formal risk assessment mechanism in the architecture. Risk is managed through: (1) Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 — anything that costs money, touches external accounts, or changes DNS is human-gated; (2) the REVIEW phase — Opus reviewers act as quality and risk gates, catching dangerous or incorrect code changes; (3) the TEST phase — test suites catch regressions and functional errors; (4) the INTEGRATE phase — the final integration check catches cross-component risks; (5) the RECON phase — codebase scanning identifies risky areas before changes begin. The approach to risk is layered (multiple phases catch different risk types) but reactive (risks are caught after code is written, not before). There is no pre-execution risk classification, no risk scoring, and no risk-based routing of tasks to different agent tiers based on risk level. The zero-overlap decomposition provides a structural risk mitigation (failures are contained within sub-objectives), but this is an architectural property, not an explicit risk mechanism. The system assumes that Opus agents (highest capability tier) handling decomposition, architecture, and review will identify and address risks through their general reasoning capability rather than through dedicated risk analysis.
unknown_handling: Handled through the RECON phase and the human operator. The RECON scouts (Haiku agents) scan the codebase to build understanding before any changes are made — this addresses known unknowns (codebase structure, existing patterns). If the system encounters truly unknown situations (technologies it does not understand, patterns it has not seen), the Fleet Doctrine mandates: (1) agents should consult the RECON data and the BATTLE-MAP for context; (2) Fleet Doctrine Rule 7: 'Grep a fleet peer before improvising on uncovered scope' — agents should seek information from other agents before guessing; (3) Fleet Doctrine Rule 8: 'Halt on gaps, never assume autonomy' — agents should stop and escalate when they encounter gaps. This is a PROBE-then-ESCALATE strategy: RECON probes the codebase to reduce unknowns, agents probe each other's work for context, and gaps are escalated to the human operator. The human operator is the ultimate unknown-handler: anything the agent fleet cannot resolve is escalated. This mirrors military doctrine — units report unknowns up the chain of command rather than improvising.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE (halt on gaps, escalate to human). Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 is explicit: 'Halt on gaps, never assume autonomy.' The default response to unknown situations is to stop and request human guidance, not to proceed and hope for the best. This is the opposite of the JORDAN approval gate's permissive default. The restrictive stance is enforced by doctrine (rules agents must follow), not by architecture (there is no hard-coded halt mechanism). The effectiveness depends on agent compliance with doctrine, which depends on the system prompt and model quality. Opus-tier agents are more likely to correctly identify gaps and halt; Sonnet and Haiku agents may miss gaps and improvise. The restrictive stance is appropriate for CommandCC's deployment model (direct human supervision) but would be impractical for fully autonomous execution.
safety_guardrails: Guardrails are primarily doctrinal (system prompt rules), not architectural: (1) Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 — halt on anything that costs money, touches external accounts, or changes DNS; (2) REVIEW phase — Opus reviewers catch safety-affecting code changes; (3) TEST phase — test suites catch regressions; (4) RECON phase is read-only — no changes during reconnaissance; (5) the zero-overlap decomposition prevents agents from conflicting (one agent cannot undo another's work); (6) the human operator is assumed to be present and monitoring — the human is the ultimate safety guardrail. Missing architectural guardrails: no sandboxing (agents operate in the same environment), no I/O firewalls, no allowlists/blocklists, no kill switch beyond human intervention, no red teaming. The system relies on Claude Code's built-in permissions system for tool access control. The guardrail model is: human sets constraints via Fleet Doctrine, Opus agents enforce doctrine through reasoning, human monitors and intervenes. This is a high-trust model appropriate for a single developer using their own tools, not for multi-tenant or production deployment.
human_decision_points: The human is IN the loop at the top level: (1) the human provides the objective (4 sentences in OPBLITZ3); (2) the human receives and reviews the BATTLE-MAP at the end; (3) the human is alerted for any Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 violations (money, accounts, DNS) and must approve; (4) the human can pause or terminate the operation at any time. The human is ON the loop during execution: the human can monitor progress (BATTLE-MAP updates, agent communications) but is not required to approve intermediate phases. The human is OFF the loop for tactical decisions: individual agents decide how to implement their sub-objectives within the constraints of the Strategy and Architecture. This mirrors the military principle of 'mission command' — the commander sets intent and constraints, subordinates execute with autonomy within those constraints. The human's cognitive load is compressed 200x: 4 sentences of intent replaces detailed task decomposition and agent coordination.
override_capability: The human operator can override at any level: (1) full halt — stop the entire operation; (2) per-phase — pause after a specific phase to review results before continuing; (3) per-agent — redirect or cancel a specific agent's work; (4) per-decision — override a Fleet Doctrine decision (approve a DNS change that the agent halted on). The human has full override authority by virtue of being the human operator at the terminal — they can Ctrl+C the process, modify agent prompts, or redirect agents. The override model is: human commands, agents execute within doctrine, human can intervene at any point. This is effective for a single-human-single-operation model but does not scale to multi-user, multi-operation deployments without additional access control.
fallback_when_no_human: CommandCC is designed for direct human supervision — it assumes the human operator is present. If the human is unavailable: (1) the operation proceeds through all phases that do not require human approval (RECON, STRATEGY, DECOMPOSE, ARCH, BUILD, WIRE, TEST, REVIEW, INTEGRATE); (2) any Fleet Doctrine Rule 8 trigger (money, accounts, DNS) halts the relevant agent until human approval; (3) other agents (unaffected by the halt) continue; (4) the BATTLE-MAP is produced with unresolved items flagged. The system does not time out waiting for human approval — it blocks on Rule 8 triggers indefinitely. The system does not auto-approve dangerous operations. This is the correct security stance: dangerous operations MUST NOT proceed without human approval. However, it means CommandCC cannot run fully autonomously — it requires a human in the loop for production deployments involving external services.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated: OPBLITZ3 — 4 human sentences → 16 agents → 4 features → 131 tests → 13 minutes wall clock. Scaling claims: 4 sentences → 12 sub-objectives → ~67 agents deployed (60 execution + 7 overhead). Maximum tested: /thunderblitz mode — 6 CC operator teammates each running internal cascades of up to 10 subagents = up to 60 agents from one terminal. The architecture scales to '128+ agents' according to the README. Theoretical limits: (1) Decomposer capacity — a single Opus agent must analyze the entire codebase and decompose it; very large codebases exceed Opus's context window and reasoning capacity; (2) parallel agent fan-out — the terminal/process model limits how many concurrent agent processes can run; (3) integration complexity — the INTEGRATE phase's Opus agent must consolidate all sub-objective results; very large agent counts produce unmanageable integration volume; (4) cost — Opus agents are expensive; 128 agents × Opus/Sonnet/Haiku inference costs become significant; (5) diminishing returns — beyond a certain parallelism, coordination overhead and integration effort outweigh the speedup from additional agents. What breaks at scale: the Decomposer becomes the bottleneck (single Opus agent cannot decompose arbitrarily large objectives); the INTEGRATE phase's Opus reviewer must understand all components (context window saturation).
parallel_execution: Parallelism is the defining architectural feature — the entire pipeline is designed around it. Within each phase, agents operate in parallel: Phase 3 (ARCH) runs multiple architects concurrently; Phase 4 (BUILD) runs multiple builders concurrently; etc. The zero-overlap Decomposer guarantee is specifically designed to enable this parallelism: because no two sub-objectives share files, they can be built/tested/reviewed in parallel without conflicts. Dependency resolution is handled by the Decomposer — all dependencies are eliminated by design (sub-objectives are fully independent). This is a radical approach to parallelism: rather than modeling dependencies (DAG-based, like JORDAN), CommandCC eliminates dependencies entirely. The trade-off is that some objectives naturally have dependencies (component A must be built before component B which uses A's API) — the Decomposer must either absorb this dependency into a single larger sub-objective (reducing parallelism) or split the dependency across phases (ARCH defines the API, BUILD-A and BUILD-B can then run in parallel using the stable API contract). The phase structure (ARCH before BUILD) provides one level of dependency management: all architects agree on interfaces during ARCH, then builders implement to those interfaces in parallel during BUILD. Parallelism quality depends entirely on Decomposer quality — a poor decomposition with hidden dependencies creates integration failures that the INTEGRATE phase must resolve.
team_scaling_model: The scaling model is linear in agent count for phases that can be parallelized, sub-linear in practice due to coordination overhead. The wave formula from the README: ~1 hour serial work → 3 waves of 4 agents = 12 total agents; ~2-3 hours → 5 waves of 4 agents = 20 total agents; ~3+ hours → 6 waves (max) of 4 agents = 24 total agents. This suggests a team size of 12-24 agents for typical operations, with /thunderblitz scaling to 60 agents. The scaling model is: add more parallel agents within a phase (wider waves) and potentially add more phases (more sequential steps). The Decomposer's output determines team size: each sub-objective spawns agents across multiple phases (ARCH, BUILD, TEST, REVIEW for that sub-objective). Team scaling is super-linear in cognitive compression: 4 human sentences → 16 agents → 200x compression, suggesting each additional sentence of human intent scales to ~4 agents → ~50x compression per sentence. The model breaks when: (1) the Decomposer cannot create enough independent sub-objectives; (2) the INTEGRATE phase cannot consolidate the growing number of component outputs; (3) cost becomes prohibitive (each agent is an LLM inference).
cost_scaling_model: Cost is primarily LLM inference tokens across the agent fleet. The cost structure: Phase 0 (RECON): N haiku scouts × cheap tokens (~30s each); Phase 1 (STRATEGY): 1 Opus × expensive tokens; Phase 2 (DECOMPOSE): 1 Opus with extended thinking × very expensive tokens; Phases 3,7,8: M Opus agents × expensive tokens; Phases 4,5,6: M Sonnet agents × moderate tokens (where M = number of sub-objectives). Total cost ≈ (RECON cost) + (2 Opus overhead) + M × (1 Opus ARCH + 1 Sonnet BUILD + 1 Sonnet WIRE + 1 Sonnet TEST + 1 Opus REVIEW) + (1 Opus INTEGRATE). Cost scales linearly with M (number of sub-objectives), which is determined by the Decomposer. The branching factor is the Decomposer's output (4-12+ sub-objectives from a single objective). For OPBLITZ3: 16 agents × average token cost × 13 minutes. The 200x cognitive compression metric suggests the human time saved (200x less human effort) is worth the token cost, even for expensive Opus calls. The Fleet Doctrine enforces cost discipline through Rule 8 (human-gated spending), but there is no per-operation cost budget or cost-tracking mechanism described in the architecture.
replanning_built_in: No global replanning — the strategy and decomposition are set at the start and not revisited. Partial replanning: (1) The REVIEW phase can send individual components back for rework (NEEDS CHANGES → BUILD phase rework); this is local replanning at the component level; (2) The INTEGRATE phase can identify and flag integration conflicts, which may require component rework; (3) If the Decomposer's output proves infeasible (sub-objectives cannot be built as decomposed), the human operator must restart the operation with modified intent — there is no automatic restructure of the decomposition. This is a significant limitation: the system cannot recover from a flawed decomposition mid-operation. The Decomposer's quality is assumed to be perfect (Opus with extended thinking), but LLMs are not infallible. The architecture trades replanning capability for speed: the sequential wave model with fixed decomposition achieves high throughput at the cost of flexibility. If an operation needs restructuring, the human restarts it — this is acceptable for a human-supervised system but limits autonomous operation.
trigger_mechanism: Partial replanning triggers: REVIEW phase PASS/NEEDS CHANGES judgment — components judged NEEDS CHANGES trigger rework (back to BUILD for the specific sub-objective). INTEGRATE phase conflict detection — integration conflicts trigger component rework. These are quality-driven triggers (component does not meet quality bar). There is no automated trigger for decomposition-level replanning — the human must recognize that the operation is not converging and restart. The Fleet Doctrine provides a manual trigger: agents encountering gaps halt and escalate to the human (Rule 7, Rule 8). The human decides whether the gap requires restarting the operation.
adaptation_cost: Component rework cost: one additional BUILD pass for the specific sub-objective (1 Sonnet inference). This is low (single agent redo, not full pipeline restart). Integration rework cost: targeted BUILD rework for conflicting components (M_affected Sonnet inferences, where M_affected is the number of conflicting components). This is moderate. Decomposition restart cost: the entire operation must restart from Phase 0 (full pipeline cost). This is high and avoided by design — the Decomposer's quality is the critical risk factor. The system optimizes for low adaptation cost at the component level (where issues are expected) at the expense of high adaptation cost at the decomposition level (where issues are assumed rare).
failure_recovery_mechanism: Layered recovery: (1) Component-level: REVIEW → NEEDS CHANGES → re-BUILD — automated quality-driven recovery; (2) Integration-level: INTEGRATE detects conflicts → targeted rework — semi-automated integration recovery; (3) Test-level: failing tests → agent revises code — automated test-driven recovery (within the BUILD phase's internal loop); (4) Fleet-level: agent encounters gap → halts → escalates to human — human-driven recovery; (5) Operation-level: operation not converging → human restarts with modified intent — human-driven recovery. Missing recovery mechanisms: no automatic retry for transient failures (network timeouts, API errors) — the system relies on Claude Code's built-in retries; no state preservation for partial results if the operation is restarted — all rework starts from scratch; no blast radius containment beyond the Decomposer's zero-overlap guarantee (a failed component does not affect other components by design). The layered recovery model is effective because it localizes failures: most issues are component-level (caught by REVIEW), some are integration-level (caught by INTEGRATE), few are decomposition-level (require human restart).
output_artifacts: The pipeline produces: Phase 0 — RECON reports (codebase structure, patterns, risks); Phase 1 — STRATEGY document (overall approach, architectural decisions); Phase 2 — DECOMPOSITION document (sub-objectives with file assignments); Phases 3-7 — per-component output (architecture docs, code, tests, review reports); Phase 8 — BATTLE-MAP.md (the final artifact — a comprehensive document showing what was built, how components relate, test results, unresolved issues, and operation metrics). The BATTLE-MAP is the primary human-facing artifact: it compresses the entire operation (16+ agents, 4+ features, 131+ tests) into a single readable document. Additional artifacts: operation metrics (agent count, features delivered, tests passed, wall clock time, cost), and all intermediate agent communications (for debugging/auditing).
briefing_format: The operation is briefed to agents through the RECON reports (codebase understanding) and the STRATEGY document (approach and constraints). The DECOMPOSITION document briefs individual agents on their specific sub-objective: which files to work on, what to build, what interface contracts to follow. The BATTLE-MAP serves as the final briefing TO the human: a structured document (markdown) covering what was done, how components relate, test results, and open issues. The format is narrative (natural language) with structured sections (component list, test summary, open issues). The military influence is visible in the terminology (RECON, STRATEGY, BATTLE-MAP) but the actual format is prose, not a military-standard format (5-paragraph order, FRAGO). The briefing is effective for a single developer but would benefit from standardization for team use.
traceability: Strong traceability within an operation: (1) Human intent → STRATEGY → DECOMPOSITION → sub-objectives → components → code changes → tests → review reports → INTEGRATION → BATTLE-MAP — the full causal chain is preserved; (2) Each component traces back to its sub-objective, which traces back to the Decomposer's decision, which traces back to the Strategy, which traces back to the human's intent; (3) Test results trace to specific code changes; (4) REVIEW judgments trace to specific component quality issues. However, traceability gaps: (a) The Decomposer's rationale for WHY a particular decomposition was chosen is not explicitly recorded — you can see WHAT was decomposed but not WHY the boundaries were drawn where they were; (b) The STRATEGY phase's rationale is Opus-internal — the strategy document captures conclusions but not the reasoning process; (c) Cross-operation traceability is absent — there is no memory of what worked in previous operations, so you cannot trace from a successful pattern in Operation N back to the decomposition approach that enabled it; (d) Agent internal reasoning is not captured — only outputs are preserved.
agent_communication_protocol: CommandCC uses Claude Code's native agent communication (the agent SDK). Within an operation: agents communicate through shared artifacts (RECON reports, STRATEGY document, BATTLE-MAP) rather than direct agent-to-agent messages. Phase transitions are managed by the orchestrator (the human or the CC framework). For multi-server deployments: the C2 Server provides a REST message bus with three message flows — DOWN (orders from command to operations), UP (reports from operations to command), and LATERAL (handoffs between peer agents). The C2 Server exposes 6 MCP tools: c2_checkin, c2_sitrep, c2_receive_orders, c2_submit_report, c2_request_resource, c2_broadcast. Agent discovery is via the C2 check-in mechanism (units check in from separate machines). This is a custom protocol, not MCP or A2A standard — it is REST-based with JSON payloads. The C2 protocol mirrors military communication: orders flow down, reports flow up, coordination flows laterally. The protocol is synchronous (request-response) with async notification (broadcast).
learning_memory_architecture: No cross-operation learning described in the available documentation. Each operation starts fresh — the system does not: (1) learn from successful decompositions to improve future Decomposer performance; (2) build a library of reusable sub-objective templates; (3) remember codebase structure from previous RECON phases to accelerate future operations on the same codebase; (4) track which agent configurations work best for which types of sub-objectives. The BATTLE-MAP from previous operations could serve as a memory artifact (documenting what was done), but the system does not automatically reference previous BATTLE-MAPs. The Fleet Doctrine Rule 7 ('Grep a fleet peer before improvising') suggests agents can consult shared context, but this is within-operation, not cross-operation. This is a significant gap for long-term use: repeated operations on the same codebase should become faster and more reliable as the system learns the codebase's structure and effective decomposition patterns. Adding cross-operation memory (a knowledge base of successful decompositions, a codebase understanding cache) would compound the 200x cognitive compression over time.
latency_bounds: Demonstrated: OPBLITZ3 achieved 13 minutes wall clock for 16 agents deploying 4 features with 131 tests. The phase structure determines latency: total time = Phase 0 (RECON, ~30s) + Phase 1 (STRATEGY) + Phase 2 (DECOMPOSE) + max(Phase 3 duration) + max(Phase 4 duration) + max(Phase 5 duration) + max(Phase 6 duration) + max(Phase 7 duration) + Phase 8 (INTEGRATE). Within parallel phases, the latency is max(agent_duration), not sum(agent_duration) — parallelism reduces wall-clock time. The 13-minute OPBLITZ3 benchmark suggests individual agent durations of 1-3 minutes per phase. The system is interactive-batch: minutes-scale latency, suitable for a developer waiting for results. Under time pressure: (1) reduce RECON depth (faster but less thorough scanning); (2) reduce agent count (fewer parallel agents, more sequential execution); (3) skip non-critical phases (e.g., skip formal ARCH phase for simple changes); (4) /thunderwave mode: 3-wave x 4-agent time-boxed variant for faster execution. The system degrades gracefully under time pressure — faster execution means less thorough analysis/testing.
evaluation_benchmarking: Evaluated through operational deployment (OPBLITZ3 proof of concept): 4 sentences of human input → 16 agents → 4 features → 131 tests → 13 minutes → 200x cognitive compression. This is a single operational demonstration, not a systematic benchmark evaluation. The system has not been evaluated against standard software engineering benchmarks (SWE-Bench, SWE-EVO, HumanEval). No comparison against other multi-agent frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph). No ablation studies (what if we remove the Decomposer? what if we use Sonnet instead of Opus for STRATEGY?). The evaluation is qualitative (it worked for one operation) rather than quantitative and reproducible. The 200x cognitive compression claim is not externally validated.
mapping_strength: Very strong mapping to JORDAN. CommandCC's 9-phase pipeline has direct analogues in JORDAN's 6-node pipeline: (1) RECON maps to research (codebase exploration); (2) STRATEGY maps to plan (high-level approach); (3) DECOMPOSE maps to plan (sub-task decomposition) — but CommandCC's Decomposer's zero-overlap guarantee is stricter than JORDAN's dependency-driven decomposition; (4) ARCH + BUILD + WIRE map to execute (implementation); (5) TEST + REVIEW map to execute + synthesize (validation and quality review); (6) INTEGRATE + BATTLE-MAP map to synthesize (consolidation of results). The 3-tier agent hierarchy (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) maps to JORDAN's model routing concept — different complexity tasks use different model capabilities. CommandCC's key innovations for JORDAN: the zero-overlap Decomposer approach to parallelism, the phase-based wave parallelism model, and the military C2 hierarchy as an organizational principle for multi-agent systems. The BATTLE-MAP artifact concept (single comprehensive output document) maps to JORDAN's synthesize node output.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN integration: (1) CommandCC's Decomposer requires zero-overlap sub-objectives — this is feasible for file-based software development but not for all JORDAN task types (research tasks, multi-modal tasks may have natural dependencies that cannot be eliminated); (2) CommandCC has NO risk assessment phase — JORDAN's risk_assessment node and approval gate fill a gap that CommandCC addresses through Fleet Doctrine (system prompts) rather than architecture; (3) CommandCC's human-in-the-loop model is 'human supervises entire operation' — JORDAN's model is 'human approves specific HIGH-risk operations' — these are different granularities; (4) CommandCC has no replanning — JORDAN's replan node is more flexible but introduces the C1 security regression (replan bypasses risk); (5) CommandCC assumes a fixed hierarchy (Opus → Sonnet → Haiku) — JORDAN's model routing is more flexible but less tested; (6) CommandCC's evaluation is a single operation demo — JORDAN needs systematic benchmarking; (7) CommandCC is tightly coupled to Claude Code's agent SDK — JORDAN runs on LangGraph + Nexus Fabric; (8) CommandCC depends on Opus's extended thinking for critical decisions (STRATEGY, DECOMPOSE, INTEGRATE) — this is expensive and creates a single point of failure; (9) CommandCC's zero-overlap approach eliminates dependencies rather than modeling them — JORDAN's DAG-based model is more flexible for tasks with natural dependencies.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Adopt CommandCC's wave-based parallel execution model — within each phase, dispatch parallel agents as a batch, collapse results before the next phase; this is cleaner than JORDAN's current mixed sequential/parallel mode; (2) Implement a dedicated Decomposer node that guarantees zero-overlap sub-objectives where possible, falling back to DAG-based decomposition for tasks with irreducible dependencies — this hybrid would give the best of both approaches; (3) Adopt the 3-tier agent hierarchy for model routing — route simple sub-tasks to Haiku-equivalent models, moderate to Sonnet-equivalent, complex to Opus-equivalent; (4) Adopt the BATTLE-MAP concept — JORDAN's synthesize node should produce a comprehensive, structured output document, not just a natural language summary; (5) Do NOT adopt CommandCC's 'no replanning' approach — JORDAN's replan node is more robust for dynamic tasks, but fix the C1 regression (replan must route through risk reassessment); (6) Do NOT adopt CommandCC's 'no risk assessment' approach — JORDAN's risk_assessment node + approval gate is essential for safety, but improve it with the Wave 1 fixes; (7) Adopt CommandCC's Fleet Doctrine concept — formalize JORDAN's operational constraints as enforceable system prompt rules rather than relying on individual node implementations; (8) Adopt CommandCC's human-as-commander model for the overall workflow — human sets intent and reviews results, rather than being a passive approval-gate operator.
validation_context: Validated through a single operational demonstration (OPBLITZ3) deploying 16 agents across 4 features with 131 passing tests in 13 minutes. The repository describes this as 'proof' of the concept. The system 'built itself using its own pattern' (self-hosting claim). Available as a public GitHub repository (Apache 2.0 license, v0.1.0). No peer-reviewed publication, no systematic benchmarks, no third-party evaluation. The validation is anecdotal (single operation, favorable conditions) rather than scientific. The repository has 23 commits on main, suggesting early-stage development.
known_failures: Inferred failure modes (not documented in available sources — the project is too new for a published failure analysis): (1) Decomposer failure — the Opus agent produces a decomposition with hidden overlaps or missed files, causing merge conflicts or missing functionality during INTEGRATE; (2) Strategy failure — the STRATEGY phase produces an approach that is architecturally invalid or incompatible with the existing codebase, causing downstream phases to produce unintegratable components; (3) RECON failure — Haiku scouts miss critical codebase structures, causing downstream agents to make incorrect assumptions about the codebase; (4) Agent compliance failure — Sonnet or Haiku agents fail to follow Fleet Doctrine, touching files outside their sub-objective, making changes that cost money, or improvising on gaps instead of halting; (5) Integration failure — the INTEGRATE phase cannot reconcile component outputs due to inconsistent assumptions made by parallel builders; (6) Test quality failure — agents write tests that pass for their component but miss cross-component interactions, giving false confidence; (7) Cost overrun — without per-operation cost budgets, an operation may consume excessive tokens (especially Opus with extended thinking for Decomposer) without producing proportional value; (8) Decomposer bottleneck — a single Opus agent is the critical path for all operations; if the Decomposer is slow or produces low-quality output, all downstream phases suffer; (9) Context window saturation — the INTEGRATE phase's Opus agent must process all component outputs; for large operations, this exceeds the context window; (10) The zero-overlap guarantee may be violated in practice — LLMs can produce overlapping file assignments despite system prompt instructions, and there is no algorithmic verification of the guarantee.
where_still_used: Active development on GitHub (github.com/dcarranza-cc/commandcc) as an open-source project (Apache 2.0). The project is in early stage (v0.1.0, 23 commits, April 2026). Primary user is the author (dcarranza-cc). The project has bootstrap URLs for easy installation into Claude Code, suggesting it is designed for practical developer use, not just research. The military C2 metaphor and 'doctrine' framing suggest the author has a military background or interest. Given the project's recency and single-contributor status, adoption is likely limited to the author and early experimenters. The approach (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku hierarchy, wave parallelism, Decomposer) may influence the broader Claude Code agent ecosystem if the project gains traction.
